claim.content: MCPAgent is a baseline agent implementation, built by adapting the OpenHands CodeAct agent, that treats tool discovery as an explicit MCP tool: the environment provides a gateway MCP server implementing find_tools(query,num_tools) and call_tool(tool_name,args); each MCP tool's JSON specification (the UTF-8 JSON serialization containing keys name (string), description (string), and inputSchema (a JSON object listing argument names, JSON types, and per-argument descriptions)) is embedded into a dense vector index, the find_tools function embeds the LLM-generated natural-language query and returns the top-k tool specifications ranked by cosine similarity between query and tool vectors, and call_tool accepts a chosen tool name and argument values and invokes the corresponding underlying MCP tool; in the reported experiments the find_tools function used dense embeddings of the tool JSON specification (the main runs used OpenAI text-embedding-3-large), returned five tools by default, allowed the LLM to iteratively generate new queries and infer dependencies during execution, and MCPAgent retained CodeAct's auxiliary tools (Think, Python, Shell, Web fetch, File edit) while replacing direct browser access with the gateway find_tools/call_tool architecture.

conclusion.content: MCPAgent is a baseline agent implementation, built by adapting the OpenHands CodeAct agent, that treats tool discovery as an explicit MCP tool: the environment provides a gateway MCP server implementing find_tools(query,num_tools) and call_tool(tool_name,args); each MCP tool's JSON specification (the UTF-8 JSON serialization containing keys name (string), description (string), and inputSchema (a JSON object listing argument names, JSON types, and per-argument descriptions)) is embedded into a dense vector index, the find_tools function embeds the LLM-generated natural-language query and returns the top-k tool specifications ranked by cosine similarity between query and tool vectors, and call_tool accepts a chosen tool name and argument values and invokes the corresponding underlying MCP tool; in the reported experiments the find_tools function used dense embeddings of the tool JSON specification (the main runs used OpenAI text-embedding-3-large), returned five tools by default, allowed the LLM to iteratively generate new queries and infer dependencies during execution, and MCPAgent retained CodeAct's auxiliary tools (Think, Python, Shell, Web fetch, File edit) while replacing direct browser access with the gateway find_tools/call_tool architecture.

premises.content: Define each MCP tool specification document d as the UTF-8 JSON string obtained by serializing the tool object with keys name (a string), description (a string), and inputSchema (a JSON object listing argument names, argument JSON types, and per-argument descriptions); define a query string q as a natural-language subtask description generated by the LLM; the retrieval procedure used by the gateway is: embed every tool-document JSON string and the query q into vectors in R^d using a text-embedding model, compute cosine similarities between the query vector and each tool vector, and rank candidate tools by descending cosine similarity; the working proposition is that the serialized JSON string representation (including the textual schema descriptions) contains the retrieval-relevant semantics so that higher cosine similarity between the embedded query and an embedded tool JSON string correlates positively with the practical usefulness of that tool for the subtask, i.e., dense retrieval over the JSON-serialized tool specifications produces rankings in which top-ranked tools are more likely to be useful for the LLM's current subgoal.

Let the retrieval system embed both tool specification documents and agent queries with an embedding model M and rank tools by cosine similarity; in the experiments reported here the main runs used OpenAI text-embedding-3-large as M; the proposition is that using text-embedding-3-large in this embedding-and-rank design yields retrieval behavior representative of the dense-specification retrieval approach (so that measured metrics like Recall@20 reflect the effectiveness of iterative LLM-generated queries under a dense-embedding retriever) and that conclusions about the benefits of allowing the LLM to iteratively generate search queries remain directionally valid beyond idiosyncrasies of this single embedding model.

The intended MCP gateway architecture prevents the LLM from directly invoking the main MCP tools by requiring use of find_tools(query,num_tools) and call_tool(name,args); during implementation the authors observed that LLMs sometimes emitted direct MCP tool calls, so to avoid runtime errors they allowed direct calls at runtime and then post-processed model outputs by rewriting direct invocations into standardized call_tool invocations before executing the underlying MCP tool; the proposition is that permitting such direct calls combined with this deterministic post-processing did not substantially change agent behavior or introduce measurable experimental artifacts compared to a strict gateway-only execution, and therefore the reported performance reflects the gateway retrieval architecture rather than artifacts of the direct-call allowance plus post-processing.

steps.reasoning: Define the implementation baseline and relevant terms: MCPAgent is the authors' baseline agent implementation; CodeAct refers to the OpenHands CodeAct agent used as the implementation base; "gateway MCP server" denotes the intermediary MCP server that exposes two functions used by the agent for tool discovery and invocation; "find_tools" denotes the gateway function that accepts a textual query and an optional num_tools parameter and returns tool specifications; "call_tool" denotes the gateway function that accepts a tool name and an argument object and executes the underlying MCP tool.

Describe the high-level architecture decision: because embedding all 18k tool specifications in the LLM context is infeasible, the authors treat tool discovery as an explicit tool by providing the LLM with the gateway MCP server exposing find_tools(query, num_tools) and call_tool(name, args). The agent must call find_tools to discover candidate tools at each step and then call call_tool to execute chosen tools. This architecture replaces providing the browser tool used by the original CodeAct agent. [Wang et al., 2024c]

Explain how the tool finder works in implementation detail: prior to running the agent the authors extract each tool's JSON specification (tool name, tool description, argument names/types/descriptions), convert each spec to a JSON string, and compute a dense embedding for each spec using a text embedding model; at runtime, find_tools encodes the agent's query with the same embedding model and returns the top-$k$ most similar tool specifications by cosine similarity. The paper provides a sample JSON tool specification used for embedding. [Table 9]

State the default retrieval parameters and embedding model used in the main experiments: by default the find_tools function returns five tools (num_tools=5) when the agent does not specify num_tools; the main experimental runs used OpenAI's text-embedding-3-large as the embedding model for the tool finder. The paper explicitly states these implementation choices in the experiments and E.1 sections. [OpenAI, 2025]

Describe how the agent invokes discovered tools: the gateway exposes a call_tool API; the agent issues a call by name and arg values and the gateway executes the corresponding MCP tool and returns outputs to the agent; the MCPAgent post-processes the LLM output to ensure tool calls are routed through the gateway if the model attempted to call tools directly.

Summarize which auxiliary tools are retained from CodeAct and which are replaced: MCPAgent retains CodeAct's standard auxiliary tools (Think, Python, Shell, Web fetch, File edit) but removes the browser tool and replaces browser-based interaction with the gateway find_tools/call_tool architecture to enable dense retrieval over JSON tool specs.

motivating_questions.content: Explicitly exposing thousands of MCP tools to an LLM in a single prompt is infeasible due to context window limits. Prior approaches either (a) preselect a small curated tool subset per task or (b) use simple retrieval from a task description, both of which fail when task descriptions and required tool specifications do not share surface similarity. A practical agent therefore needs an in-execution tool discovery mechanism that (i) presents only a manageable set of tool specifications per step, (ii) encodes tool documentation so retrieval can operate over name/description/argument metadata, and (iii) enables the LLM to iteratively search and call tools while reasoning about intermediate results. Implementing retrieval as an explicit MCP tool (find_tools) that returns full tool specifications resolves the context-size bottleneck and lets the agent perform stepwise discovery and orchestration.

paper:1188121281946452023.authors: Reza Esfandiarpoor | Vishwas Suryanarayanan | Stephen H. Bach | Vishal Chowdhary | Anthony Aue
paper:1188121281946452023.en_title: TheMCPCompany: Creating General-purpose Agents with Task-specific Tools
paper:1188121281946452023.zh_title: MCP公司：使用特定任务工具创建通用智能体
